model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"With a fixed budget of 256 steps (about one-third of an epoch at batch size 64), the current LR is likely conservative for rapid early progress. Increasing Adam LR should improve within-budget loss reduction with minimal complexity and low contract risk.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"USE_BATCHNORM = False","old":"USE_BATCHNORM = True","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"In this fixed-step regime, BatchNorm statistics may be poorly estimated and can destabilize early optimization in an MLP. Disabling BatchNorm simplifies the network dynamics and often improves short-budget convergence on flattened CIFAR-10 inputs.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"USE_BATCHNORM = True","old":"USE_BATCHNORM = False","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Both recent changes hurt validation loss, so the highest-probability recovery is to restore the better-known regime: lower Adam LR with BatchNorm enabled. This resets optimization dynamics to the previously strongest visible configuration before testing new ideas.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = True","old":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = False","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    total_steps = TRAIN_MAX_STEPS if TRAIN_MAX_STEPS is not None else steps_per_epoch * 100","old":"    total_steps = steps_per_epoch * 100","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"Enable a meaningful within-budget cosine decay by tying scheduler horizon to fixed training steps. This can improve final validation loss in short runs by allowing larger early updates with smoother late-step refinement.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = False","old":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = True","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"Recent cosine scheduling degraded this short-budget run, so disable it and test AdamW as a cleaner optimizer-family change. AdamW often yields better early generalization than Adam at the same learning rate and decay settings.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-5","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"With only 256 optimization steps, strong decay can underfit early. Reducing AdamW weight decay keeps regularization but allows faster useful fit within the fixed-step budget.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 7e-4","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"After switching to AdamW, a modest LR increase may improve early progress within the fixed 256-step budget without the instability seen at 1e-3. This is a minimal, targeted probe around the current best configuration.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 7e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Restore the better LR and test a larger batch under fixed-step evaluation to expose more training examples per run. This is a direct budget-allocation tweak that can improve validation loss without adding model complexity.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strong gain from increasing batch size suggests this fixed-step setting benefits from processing more examples per update budget. Testing 256 continues that direction with a minimal single-parameter change.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Performance improved substantially as batch size increased, which aligns with fixed-step evaluation where more samples per step can dominate. Testing 512 is the next minimal extension of that trend.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Validation loss keeps improving as batch size scales up under fixed-step evaluation, so increasing to 1024 is the most direct continuation of the strongest observed trend while keeping all other settings stable.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 7e-4","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"With batch size now at 1024, the optimizer can typically support a higher learning rate. A moderate LR increase is a low-complexity way to test better optimization speed at this larger-batch regime.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 2048","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Batch scaling has been the strongest lever so far in fixed-step mode. Moving from 1024 to 2048 directly tests whether additional sample throughput per step yields further loss reduction.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 7e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"At batch size 2048, optimization can often tolerate and benefit from a higher learning rate. Raising LR to 1e-3 is a targeted large-batch scaling test without increasing runtime further.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.99)","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.999)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"With very large batch and short training horizon, lowering AdamW beta2 can make second-moment estimates adapt faster, which may improve early optimization and final fixed-step loss.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"After strong optimization gains, the model may now be capacity-limited. Increasing base width is a minimal architecture change that can improve fit while keeping the training loop and optimizer unchanged.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The recent width increase improved loss, suggesting the model is still capacity-limited. A further modest width bump is a direct continuation of the best-performing trend with minimal code change.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Width increases continue to help, so adding one more hidden block is a natural next capacity step. This keeps the successful optimizer/batch regime unchanged while testing whether extra depth improves representation quality.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 8e-4","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"After increasing model depth, a slightly lower learning rate may improve stability and final loss while preserving the successful large-batch regime.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 8e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The last LR decrease slightly worsened validation loss, so revert to 1e-3, which is the better-performing rate for the current deep, wide, large-batch AdamW configuration.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}
